voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Andes Mountains of South America — high grasslands and farms
conservation: Domesticated; wild ancestor (vicuña) is near threatened
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its large ears stand upright and turn toward new sounds.
Its eyes give it a wide view to spot movement across open slopes.
It uses its nose to recognize herd members and detect unfamiliar animals.
Sensitive nostrils help it identify plants before it eats them.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives on high mountain grasslands called puna in the Andes.
Elevations can range from 3,000 to 5,000 meters above sea level.
Tough, low-growing grasses are its main food in this cold, thin-air environment.
It lives in herds, which provides warmth and safety on exposed slopes.
How does it send messages?: It hums quietly to communicate with herd members while grazing.
A sharp alarm call alerts the whole herd when danger is spotted.
It spits a mouthful of stomach contents at animals that get too close.
Ear position and neck posture show whether it is relaxed or tense.
What does it do when something changes?: When alarmed, the whole herd groups tightly together.
It orients its body toward the threat and watches before moving.
In cold weather, the herd huddles close for shared warmth.
It avoids unfamiliar ground and tends to retrace familiar paths.
How does it learn?: A baby alpaca stays close to its mother and copies her grazing habits.
Young alpacas learn which plants to eat by watching adults in the herd.
Crias learn herd alarm signals by responding to adult calls early in life.
How does it have babies?: A female gives birth to one cria after a gestation of about eleven months.
Crias are born standing within an hour of birth.
The mother and cria hum to each other constantly after birth to bond.
Crias nurse for six to eight months and stay near the mother.
What are its limits?: High altitude cold can be dangerous for young crias if shelter is lacking.
Its mountain grass diet does not supply enough water on its own.
Predators such as pumas can separate and take a cria from the herd.
Sudden storms at altitude can drive the herd to lower ground quickly.
What does this teach us?: Its thick coat traps air and holds warmth where temperatures drop fast.
Living in a herd at altitude means every animal depends on the group.
What looks like simple grazing is a carefully balanced search for food and safety.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Domesticated from the wild vicuña in the Andes thousands of years ago.
Physiologically adapted to high-altitude, low-oxygen mountain grasslands.
Lives in herds where alarm signaling protects all animals simultaneously.
Crias are typically born standing and ready to walk within an hour.
still_debated: How much of current herd behavior is domestication vs. inherited wild vicuña patterns.
Whether spit signaling is primarily conflict de-escalation or dominance enforcement.
How well individual alpacas recognize humans vs. other alpacas.
common_failure_modes: Confusing alpacas with llamas (related, but distinct domesticated species).
Assuming alpacas can thrive at low elevations without management adjustments.
Treating the whole herd as interchangeable; alpacas have strong individual bonds.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not call an alpaca a llama or a vicuña.
Do not describe it as 'wild' — it is a domestic species.
Do not imply it is solitary; herd living is required for well-being.
